title: Whether expats writing about a host city owe it love or honesty
summary: Argue whether foreigners writing about the city they live in should foreground affection or unflinching honesty when their account will be read by both natives and outsiders.
topic: Whether foreign writers writing about their host city should choose love or honesty
prompt: Write a 350–500-word argumentative essay about whether a foreign writer living in a host city — Hue, Hanoi, Tashkent, Tbilisi — should foreground their affection for the place when they write about it, or whether honest criticism (of corruption, of cruelty, of inertia) is part of what the host city is owed in return for hospitality. Use a 'what' cleft, a concession ('Granted, …' or 'Admittedly, …'), at least one passive construction, and one participle clause. Anchor the argument in a specific city or kind of writing. Take a clear position. Vietnam — foreigner's lens only.
hintStarters: What a foreign writer owes the city they live in is something rarely addressed in the polished essays they tend to…
Granted, the foreign writer who casts every observation about their host city as a complaint can drift into a kind of tourist superiority that…
Reading a recent essay about Hue by a long-term British resident, I noticed how carefully it avoided naming anything that might have…

phóng viên nước ngoài
cư dân lâu dài
làm dịu một quan sát
cái nhìn của người ngoài
tâng bốc chủ nhà của mình
viết thành bài đăng
văn xuôi mang tính ngoại giao
tình cảm thiếu phê phán

hintVocab.phrase: to be read by both natives and outsiders
hintVocab.meaningVi: được đọc bởi cả người bản xứ lẫn người ngoài
hồi ký của người sống xa quê
văn học hậu du khách
ghi chép mà không sở hữu
tư cách bình luận đã giành được
nói thay ý kiến của người bản xứ
một chi tiết trung thực
thay thế tâm trạng cho lập luận
trí tuệ được chấp nhận / quan điểm phổ biến
mô tả không khí / cảnh quan tinh thần
sự trung thực dân sự
đặt sự hoan nghênh của mình vào rủi ro

sampleText:
Reading a recent essay about Hue by a long-term British resident, I noticed how carefully it avoided naming anything that might have offended the city it claimed to love. The rivers were beautiful. The food was honest. The people were warm. The corruption that had reshaped half the riverfront in the past decade was not mentioned. The essay was perfectly pleasant. It was also, in a way that the writer probably did not intend, a kind of postcard.

What a foreign writer owes the city they live in is something rarely addressed in the polished essays they tend to publish about it. The unspoken assumption is that affection is the proper register for the outsider, and that anything sharper would be ungrateful — a foreigner abusing their welcome. The result is a small genre of expat writing in which warm impressions are exchanged for warm reception, and in which nothing genuinely contested ever gets said.

Granted, the foreign writer who casts every observation about their host city as a complaint can drift into a kind of tourist superiority that helps nobody. There is a long, embarrassing history of foreigners writing about places they barely understand as if their three years of residence and a half-learned vocabulary gave them standing to judge entire systems. The case for honest criticism is not the case for the loud, ignorant foreigner who treats a host country as material for a personal essay.

Even so, the more common failure in expat writing is the opposite one. The writer who has lived in Hue, Tashkent, or Hanoi for fifteen years, learned the language well enough to argue in it, watched the same neighbourhood get bulldozed three times, and still produces only river-and-food prose has not, by their reticence, honoured the place. They have flattered it. The hosts who read that prose in translation can usually tell which is which.

Admittedly, honesty for a foreign writer carries different stakes than honesty for a local one. A local writer who criticises the city criticises their own; a foreign writer who does the same is making a claim — implicit but real — about their right to weigh in. That standing has to be earned. It is earned, when it is earned at all, by long residence, real language, careful reading, and the willingness to be wrong in print.

What a foreign writer finally owes their host city is neither uncritical affection nor a tourist's complaint. It is the discipline of writing about it the way one writes about somewhere one expects to live for the rest of one's life — honestly, particularly, with affection for what deserves affection and clear sight for what does not. The city, after all, is not a hotel. It is a place where other people also have to live with what one writes.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft to expose a writerly silence
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What a foreign writer owes the city they live in is something rarely addressed in the polished essays they tend to publish about it' dùng cấu trúc 'What … is …' để chỉ ra một câu hỏi đạo đức mà thể loại đang xét bỏ qua. Trạng từ 'rarely' đặt một phán xét nhẹ nhưng rõ ràng vào câu.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What a foreign writer owes the city they live in is something rarely addressed in the polished essays they tend to publish about it' uses a what-cleft to name an ethical question the genre skips over. The adverb 'rarely' places a soft but unmistakable judgement into the sentence.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Semicolon-balanced contrast for moral redefinition
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'has not, by their reticence, honoured the place. They have flattered it' dùng hai câu ngắn liên tiếp với cấu trúc song song để tái định nghĩa hành động của nhà văn — từ 'tôn vinh' (mà nhà văn nghĩ mình đang làm) sang 'tâng bốc' (mà bài luận thực sự là). Sự đối lập sắc bén này thường được dùng ở B2 để tạo ra một bước ngoặt đạo đức.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'has not, by their reticence, honoured the place. They have flattered it' uses two short, parallel sentences to redefine the writer's act — from 'honouring' (which the writer thinks they are doing) to 'flattering' (which the essay actually is). This sharp contrast is a B2 staple for moral pivots.

sampleTranslationVi:
Đọc một bài luận gần đây về Huế bởi một cư dân Anh sống lâu dài, tôi nhận thấy bài viết đã cẩn thận như thế nào trong việc tránh nêu tên bất cứ điều gì có thể đã xúc phạm thành phố mà nó tuyên bố yêu mến. Các con sông thì đẹp. Thức ăn thì trung thực. Người dân thì ấm áp. Sự tham nhũng đã định hình lại một nửa bờ sông trong thập kỷ qua không được nhắc đến. Bài luận hoàn toàn dễ chịu. Nó cũng là, theo một cách mà người viết có lẽ không có ý định, một loại bưu thiếp.

Điều mà một nhà văn nước ngoài nợ thành phố nơi họ sống là điều hiếm khi được đề cập trong các bài luận được trau chuốt mà họ có xu hướng xuất bản về nó. Giả định không lời là tình cảm là giọng điệu phù hợp cho người ngoài, và rằng bất cứ điều gì sắc hơn sẽ là vô ơn — một người nước ngoài lạm dụng sự chào đón của mình. Kết quả là một thể loại nhỏ của văn viết của người sống xa quê trong đó ấn tượng ấm áp được trao đổi để có sự đón tiếp ấm áp, và trong đó không gì thực sự gây tranh cãi được nói ra.

Thừa nhận rằng, nhà văn nước ngoài đặt mọi quan sát về thành phố sở tại của họ thành một lời phàn nàn có thể trôi dạt vào một loại sự ưu việt của du khách chẳng giúp ích cho ai. Có một lịch sử dài, đáng xấu hổ của những người nước ngoài viết về những nơi họ hầu như không hiểu như thể ba năm cư trú của họ và một vốn từ vựng học được nửa vời cho họ tư cách để phán xét toàn bộ hệ thống. Lập luận cho phê phán trung thực không phải là lập luận cho người nước ngoài ồn ào, vô tri đối xử với đất nước sở tại như tài liệu cho một bài luận cá nhân.

Dẫu vậy, sự thất bại phổ biến hơn trong văn viết của người sống xa quê là sai lầm ngược lại. Nhà văn đã sống ở Huế, Tashkent, hay Hà Nội mười lăm năm, đã học ngôn ngữ đủ tốt để tranh luận bằng nó, đã xem cùng một khu phố bị san phẳng ba lần, và vẫn chỉ sản xuất văn xuôi về sông và thức ăn không, qua sự kín tiếng của họ, tôn vinh nơi đó. Họ đã tâng bốc nó. Các chủ nhà đọc văn xuôi đó qua bản dịch thường có thể phân biệt được cái nào là cái nào.

Thừa nhận rằng, sự trung thực đối với một nhà văn nước ngoài mang theo những rủi ro khác với sự trung thực đối với một nhà văn bản xứ. Một nhà văn bản xứ phê phán thành phố là phê phán của chính họ; một nhà văn nước ngoài làm điều tương tự đang đưa ra một tuyên bố — ngầm nhưng thực sự — về quyền lên tiếng của họ. Tư cách đó phải được giành lấy. Nó được giành, khi nó được giành chút nào, bởi sự cư trú lâu dài, ngôn ngữ thực sự, đọc cẩn thận, và sự sẵn lòng sai trên bản in.

Điều mà một nhà văn nước ngoài cuối cùng nợ thành phố sở tại của họ không phải là tình cảm thiếu phê phán cũng không phải là lời phàn nàn của du khách. Đó là kỷ luật viết về nó theo cách người ta viết về một nơi mà người ta dự định sống suốt phần đời còn lại của mình — một cách trung thực, cụ thể, với tình cảm cho điều xứng đáng được yêu và cái nhìn rõ ràng cho điều không xứng đáng. Thành phố, suy cho cùng, không phải là một khách sạn. Đó là một nơi mà những người khác cũng phải sống với những gì người ta viết.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft to expose a writerly silence?
options: Polished essays rarely addressed they tend to publish about it what a foreign writer owes the city they live in is something.
What a foreign writer owes the city they live in is something rarely addressed in the polished essays they tend to publish about it.
Rarely addressed in the polished essays they tend to publish what a foreign writer owes the city is something they live in.
A foreign writer what owes the city they live in is something rarely addressed in the polished essays about it they tend to publish.
mcQuestions.explanation: The cleft 'What … is …' delays the evaluation ('something rarely addressed in the polished essays they tend to publish about it') to the emphatic position.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What … is …' with the writer's diagnosis placed after 'is'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'earned standing to comment' mean?
options: The legal right to comment on internet forums
The accumulated credibility — through residence, language, reading, and risk — that gives a foreign writer the right to make a serious judgement about the place they live in
A formal qualification awarded by a literary academy
The physical posture required for serious writing
mcQuestions.explanation: Standing in this sense is the right to speak meaningfully; the essay insists it has to be earned through long residence, real language, careful reading, and willingness to be wrong in print.
mcQuestions.hint: What does 'standing' mean in the sense of 'the right to speak on a subject', and what 'earns' it?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'granted' correctly as a concession?
options: Granted, the foreign writer who casts every observation about their host city as a complaint can drift into a kind of tourist superiority that helps nobody.
The foreign writer granted who casts every observation as a complaint about their host city tourist superiority into a kind of drift can helps nobody.
Into a kind of tourist superiority granted that helps nobody every observation as a complaint about their host city the foreign writer who casts can drift.
About their host city as a complaint the foreign writer who casts every observation granted can drift into a kind of tourist superiority that helps nobody.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted,' followed by a complete clause cleanly grants a real point to the opposing argument.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option places 'Granted,' before a full, grammatical sentence?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which passive construction correctly foregrounds the exchange happening in expat writing?
options: Warm impressions are exchanged for warm reception.
Exchanged for warm reception warm impressions are.
Warm impressions exchange warm reception are for.
For warm reception warm impressions are exchanged.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'are exchanged for' is a passive construction that places 'warm impressions' in subject position and reveals an implicit transaction without naming an agent.
mcQuestions.hint: Which sentence places 'warm impressions' as the subject of the passive verb 'are exchanged'?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to ventriloquise local opinion' mean?
options: To learn to imitate local accents perfectly
To speak as if one were giving voice to what local people think, when in fact one is putting one's own words into their mouths
To attend a ventriloquism performance with locals
To translate local opinion polls into a foreign language
mcQuestions.explanation: Ventriloquism is throwing one's voice through another speaker. The metaphor names a recurring danger for foreign writers: producing 'local' opinion that is actually their own.
mcQuestions.hint: What does a ventriloquist do — and what would it mean to do that with someone else's opinion?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best paraphrases 'They have flattered it'?
options: They have offered honest praise where praise was due.
They have written about it in a way that pleases rather than tells the truth — overstating affection while leaving out hard realities.
They have iron-pressed the city's reputation.
They have refused to compliment the city at all.
mcQuestions.explanation: Flattery, in the essay's usage, is the opposite of honest affection — a smooth, untruthful tribute the writer offers because it is socially safer than honesty.
mcQuestions.hint: What is the essay contrasting — honouring against what kind of opposite?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to risk one's welcome' mean for a foreign writer?
options: To wear out one's visa by overstaying
To write something honest enough that one's social position in the host country could plausibly become uncomfortable
To refuse hospitality offered by locals
To organise an unwelcoming public event
mcQuestions.explanation: Risking one's welcome is the price of honest writing in a place where one is a guest: one risks the comfortable social acceptance that uncritical affection more easily secures.
mcQuestions.hint: What is being 'risked', and what is the cost of telling the truth?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a participle clause as an opener to set the analytical scene?
options: Reading a recent essay about Hue by a long-term British resident, I noticed how carefully it avoided naming anything that might have offended the city it claimed to love.
I noticed reading a recent essay about Hue by a long-term British resident how carefully it avoided naming anything that might have offended the city it claimed to love.
The city it claimed to love a recent essay reading about Hue by a long-term British resident I noticed how carefully it avoided naming anything that might have offended.
Avoided naming anything reading a recent essay about Hue by a long-term British resident that might have offended the city it claimed to love how carefully I noticed.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Reading a recent essay about Hue by a long-term British resident' is a present-participle clause opener — it sets the analytical scene before the main clause delivers the observation.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for an -ing phrase at the start of the sentence, separated by a comma from the main clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best signals a return to the main argument after a concession?
options: For example,
Even so, the more common failure in expat writing is the opposite one.
On the contrary, no foreigner has ever written badly.
Furthermore, all foreign writers are heroes.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so' grants the previous concession (the loud, ignorant foreigner is real) and pivots to the writer's main argument that the opposite failure is more common.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase both accepts the concession and reasserts the writer's broader position?

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's central position?
options: Foreign writers should write only sweet, flattering essays about their host cities.
Foreign writers should write only sharp, complaining essays about their host cities.
Foreign writers owe their host city neither uncritical affection nor tourist complaint, but the discipline of honest, particular, long-residence writing earned through real engagement.
Foreign writers should never write about their host cities at all.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay defines the duty between two strawmen: not flattery, not complaint, but earned honesty rooted in real residence and language.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at paragraphs five and six for the writer's specific definition of what is actually owed.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence maintains the most formal academic register?
options: Honestly expat writing is basically a bunch of sweet nothings, no question.
It's kind of obvious that foreigners writing about a city should just be honest, full stop.

The convention by which long-resident foreign writers exchange unchallenging affection for hospitable reception has produced a thin sub-genre whose primary effect is to leave contested questions unaddressed.
Foreigners writing about their host city are pretty much always too sweet, you know?
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C uses formal vocabulary ('convention by which', 'thin sub-genre', 'contested questions unaddressed') and avoids contractions, slang, and informal markers.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid 'honestly', 'basically', 'kind of', 'full stop', 'you know', and contractions.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to substitute mood for argument' mean in writing?
options: To write only when in a good mood
To rely on atmosphere and feeling to carry an essay rather than make a claim and defend it with evidence
To use mood music as background while writing
To use grammatical mood (subjunctive, indicative) for emphasis
mcQuestions.explanation: When mood substitutes for argument, the essay produces feeling without a defended claim — exactly the river-and-food prose the essay criticises.
mcQuestions.hint: What is the difference between writing that creates a mood and writing that makes an argument?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a defining relative clause?
options: A local writer who criticises the city criticises their own; a foreign writer who does the same is making a claim — implicit but real — about their right to weigh in.
Who criticises the city a local writer criticises their own a foreign writer who does the same is making a claim implicit but real about their right to weigh in.
Is making a claim implicit but real about their right to weigh in a local writer who criticises the city criticises their own a foreign writer who does the same.
Their right to weigh in a foreign writer who does the same is making a claim about a local writer who criticises the city criticises their own implicit but real.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'who criticises the city' and 'who does the same' are defining relative clauses that specify exactly which writer the essay means in each half of the contrast.
mcQuestions.hint: Defining relative clauses follow the noun directly and start with 'who', 'that', or 'which'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'the discipline of writing about it the way one writes about somewhere one expects to live for the rest of one's life' mean?
options: Writing under a strict daily schedule
Writing about the host city as a permanent home rather than as a passing experience — with the seriousness, care, and accountability that long commitment requires
Writing for one's own benefit only
Writing only after one has lived somewhere for the rest of one's life
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay's standard is treating the host city as a place one will not leave — which forces honesty, particularity, and accountability into the writing.
mcQuestions.hint: What changes about how one writes when one expects to live with the consequences forever?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best closes the essay by stating what the city is — and is not?
options: In conclusion, writing about foreign cities is hard.
Therefore, all expat writing must be banned.
The city, after all, is not a hotel. It is a place where other people also have to live with what one writes.
To sum up, both affection and honesty are useful in essays.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C closes with a sharp, memorable contrast (hotel versus inhabited place) that captures the essay's central moral claim about accountability.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option ends with a specific contrast that locates the writer's accountability?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay say earns a foreign writer the standing to criticise their host city?
options: A passport from a powerful country
Long residence, real language, careful reading, and the willingness to be wrong in print
A formal degree in journalism
A signed agreement with a local publisher
mcQuestions.explanation: Paragraph five names exactly these four — long residence, real language, careful reading, willingness to be wrong in print — as what earns the right to weigh in.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at paragraph five for the writer's specific list of what earns standing.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a piece of writing — an essay, a column, a book — that a foreign resident has produced about a city you know. Did it lean toward uncritical affection or toward sharp complaint? What would you ask the writer to change before publishing it again, and what would your edit say about who you think the writing was really for?
